skill.name: logprob-prefill-analysis
skill.description: Guides reward hacking prefill sensitivity analysis by running token trajectory, logprob, and integrated comparison workflows for model checkpoints.
skill.summary: Analyze model checkpoint susceptibility with prefill token and logprob metrics.
skill.author: EleutherAI

security_audit.summary: Static external command findings are mostly Markdown examples for running local analysis scripts; they indicate real command-execution behavior but not malicious code. The hardcoded URLs point to a localhost vLLM endpoint, and the weak cryptography blocker at line 3 is a false positive from descriptive text. Publish with a warning because the skill asks agents to run shell and Python commands, write experiment artifacts, and terminate a local vLLM process.

medium_findings.title: Agent-Run Shell and Python Pipeline Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill directs agents to run repository Python scripts and shell commands for model evaluation. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it can execute unreviewed local code and consume GPU or CPU resources.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple bash code blocks explicitly invoke Python scripts and vLLM commands. The context is an analysis pipeline, so the risk is operational rather than evidence of malware.

medium_findings.title: Process Termination Command in Batch Example
medium_findings.description: The batch example uses a broad process match to stop vLLM. If run without review, it could terminate unrelated local vLLM processes owned by the user.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is directly present in a shell example and has a broad process pattern. It is still scoped to vLLM and appears intended for cleanup.

low_findings.title: Localhost Network URL Static Finding Dismissed
low_findings.description: The detected URLs target a local vLLM API endpoint. No evidence found of external network exfiltration or contact with third-party services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both URL findings use http://localhost:8000/v1 in evaluation examples. They do not identify an external host or suspicious destination.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Finding Dismissed
low_findings.description: The blocker alert points to the description line about reward hacking and exploitability. No evidence found of weak cryptographic algorithms or cryptographic operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is frontmatter prose and contains no crypto function, algorithm name, or implementation. The static signal is a semantic false positive.

low_findings.title: Experiment Metadata May Expose Local Details
low_findings.description: The run context records command arguments, git commit, Python version, CUDA information, pip freeze, and environment details. This can reveal local setup information if generated artifacts are shared.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly lists metadata fields that may include local environment details. The exact sensitivity depends on what the referenced scripts record.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown Command Blocks Execute Local Tooling
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains bash examples that run analysis scripts and model-serving commands. Agents should ask for confirmation and inspect the repository scripts before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples contain direct shell invocations that an agent could execute. They are transparent Markdown examples, not hidden code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Process Kill Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The cleanup example uses process-name matching to stop vLLM. This is useful cleanup behavior but can affect unrelated matching processes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The process termination command is explicit and easy to verify in the batch example. The risk is bounded to matching vLLM processes.

content.value_statement: Model evaluators need consistent ways to measure reward hacking susceptibility across checkpoints. This skill guides token, logprob, and integrated analyses with reproducible experiment outputs.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to run a full prefill sensitivity analysis pipeline from one orchestration command.
Documents checkpoint discovery from sensitivity experiment configuration files.
Guides token-based trajectory analysis across tested prefill levels.
Guides prefill logprob computation for single checkpoints and batch runs.
Explains integrated comparison of token accessibility and logprob metrics.
Lists expected result files, plots, and run context artifacts.
limitations: Requires existing model checkpoints, prefill source data, and project scripts.
Assumes vLLM, CUDA, and related Python packages are already installed.
Does not provide the analysis scripts or datasets by itself.
Reference results may not apply to different models or problem sets.

use_cases.title: Audit Checkpoint Susceptibility
use_cases.description: Compare how different training checkpoints respond to exploit-oriented prefills.

use_cases.title: Reproduce Evaluation Artifacts
use_cases.description: Run the documented workflow to regenerate trajectory tables, logprob files, and comparison plots.

use_cases.title: Compare Metric Predictiveness
use_cases.description: Assess whether token thresholds or logprob sums better predict future exploitability.

prompt_templates.title: Review Requirements
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup check before running any commands.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the logprob-prefill-analysis skill to list the prerequisites for my current repository. Identify missing checkpoints, data files, and tools before running anything.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Dry Run
prompt_templates.scenario: Prepare a safe first execution plan.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the logprob-prefill-analysis skill to plan a dry run for the latest prefill sensitivity experiment. Show the commands and expected outputs without executing them.

prompt_templates.title: Run Trajectory Analysis
prompt_templates.scenario: Execute the token-based stage for a known experiment.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the logprob-prefill-analysis skill to run token-based trajectory analysis for my selected run directory. Summarize generated tables and plots.

prompt_templates.title: Integrate Logprob Metrics
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced comparison of token and logprob predictors.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the logprob-prefill-analysis skill to compute logprobs, merge them with trajectory data, and compare predictive power across prefill levels.

output_examples.input: Analyze the latest sensitivity run with the full pipeline.
output: Identified the most recent sensitivity experiment and checkpoint list.
Ran trajectory, logprob, and integrated analysis stages.
Reported generated result directories, plots, and key metric comparisons.

output_examples.input: Explain why no logprob rows were merged.
output: Checked whether trajectory prefill levels match available logprob directories.
Flagged missing prefill levels or mismatched run names.
Suggested rerunning the affected logprob stage with aligned inputs.

output_examples.input: Compare token threshold and logprob threshold results.
output: Summarized which problems reached each threshold.
Compared predictor strength and timing differences.
Highlighted cases where one metric fired earlier than the other.

best_practices: Run a dry run first to confirm checkpoint and prefill paths.
Inspect referenced scripts before allowing an agent to execute commands.
Keep run context artifacts with results for reproducibility.
anti_patterns: Running the batch script without checking active vLLM processes.
Mixing trajectory data and logprob outputs from different experiments.
Comparing mean logprob when sequence probability is the intended metric.

faq.question: What does this skill analyze?
faq.answer: It analyzes how prefilled exploit reasoning affects model behavior across training checkpoints.

faq.question: Does it run models directly?
faq.answer: It documents commands that run local project scripts and vLLM servers when the user approves execution.

faq.question: What data do I need?
faq.answer: You need model checkpoints, prefill source examples, sensitivity run outputs, and the referenced analysis scripts.

faq.question: Why compare token and logprob metrics?
faq.answer: Token thresholds show accessibility, while logprob sums estimate how natural exploit reasoning is to the model.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, or Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill gives procedural guidance that these coding agents can follow in a prepared repository.

faq.question: What should I review before publishing outputs?
faq.answer: Review metadata files for local environment details, package lists, paths, and command arguments.
